questions.q: A primigravida is a woman experiencing pregnancy for what time?
How many weeks does a typical human pregnancy last from conception to birth?
What is the umbilical cord's primary function during pregnancy?
choices: Prevents the baby from moving too much
Connects the baby to the placenta for nutrients and oxygen
Protects the baby from loud noises

questions.q: At approximately 6 months of pregnancy, a developing baby is roughly the size of which fruit?
What does LMP stand for in pregnancy tracking?
What is the soft, diamond-shaped spot on a newborn's head called?
Morning sickness during pregnancy is most commonly experienced during which trimester?
An ultrasound during pregnancy primarily allows doctors to do what?
choices: Visualize the baby and monitor development
Deliver medication directly to the fetus

questions.q: What is colostrum?
choices: The thick, nutrient-rich first milk produced after birth
A surgical instrument used during delivery

questions.q: At 9 months of pregnancy, a developing baby is approximately the size of which fruit?
What is the cervix's role during pregnancy and labor?
choices: It produces hormones to maintain pregnancy
It acts as the gateway between the uterus and birth canal

questions.q: A cesarean section involves which medical procedure?
choices: Surgical removal of the baby through an incision in the abdomen
Breaking the amniotic sac to speed up labor
Applying pressure to the mother's abdomen

questions.q: What is the APGAR score used to assess?
choices: The mother's pain level during labor
The baby's health and vitals immediately after birth

questions.q: When does ovulation occur in the menstrual cycle?
choices: Approximately 14 days before the next menstruation

questions.q: What is the placenta's primary function during pregnancy?
choices: To exchange oxygen, nutrients, and waste between mother and baby

questions.q: How many stages of labor are there?
An embryo becomes classified as a fetus after how many weeks of pregnancy?
Pre-eclampsia during pregnancy is characterized by which symptoms?
choices: High blood pressure and protein in the urine

questions.q: What is the amniotic sac filled with?
choices: Amniotic fluid that cushions and protects the baby

questions.q: At approximately 3 months of pregnancy, a developing baby is roughly the size of which fruit?
What is the term for when a baby is positioned with the buttocks or feet down instead of head down in the womb?
How many trimesters make up a typical pregnancy?
What is the yellowish fluid that is produced before regular breast milk comes in?
What does the acronym NICU stand for in hospital settings?
At approximately how many weeks of pregnancy can a fetal heartbeat typically be detected with a Doppler?
What is the medical term for the opening of the cervix during labor?
What is the name of the birth partner who provides continuous physical and emotional support during labor but is not a medical professional?
What does EDD stand for when discussing pregnancy timelines?
Which two female reproductive organs are responsible for producing eggs each month?
What is the term for the stage of pregnancy immediately after a baby is born?
At one month of gestation, a developing baby is approximately the size of which tiny object?
What is the name of the medical procedure that uses an instrument resembling kitchen tongs to assist in guiding the baby out during delivery?
What is the hormone that signals the body to produce and release milk during breastfeeding?
What is a bouncy inflatable ball used during labor to aid comfort and positioning called?
After 8 weeks of development, what is the medical term for the developing baby in the womb?
What is the scoring system named after Dr. Virginia Apgar that assesses a newborn's health immediately after birth?
What is the term for intense, rhythmic muscle contractions of the uterus that help push the baby down during labor?
What is the thick organ that develops in the uterus to supply oxygen and nutrients to the developing baby?
At 2 months of gestation, a developing baby is approximately the size of which fruit?
What is the medical term for the soft spot on a baby's skull that allows the head to compress during birth?
What is the name of the reflex where a startled newborn throws their arms outward as if catching themselves from falling?
During which trimester of pregnancy does the baby typically become viable outside the womb?
What is the name of the surgical procedure in which a baby is delivered through an incision in the mother's abdomen?
What is the term for the premature breaking of the amniotic sac during pregnancy?
What is the name of the pain relief gas mixture commonly used during labor that contains nitrous oxide?
What is the medical term for a woman experiencing her first pregnancy?
What is the term for the stage of labor that begins when the cervix is fully dilated and ends with the birth of the baby?
What is the name of the creamy white substance that covers and protects a newborn's skin in the womb?
What is the common name for the nausea and vomiting experienced by many pregnant women in early pregnancy?
What does the abbreviation EDD stand for in prenatal care?
What is the name of the organ that develops during pregnancy to nourish the growing baby?
What is the medical term for when a baby is positioned head-down in the uterus, ready for vaginal birth?
What is the name of the fluid-filled sac that surrounds and protects the developing baby during pregnancy?
What is the term for the final stage of labor that involves the delivery of the placenta?
What is the name of the tiny white bumps that sometimes appear on a newborn's nose and face, caused by blocked oil glands?
What is the abbreviation for the date of the last menstrual period, which is used to calculate pregnancy duration?
How many months does a typical human pregnancy last?
What is the medical term for the first three months of pregnancy?
At what week of gestation does the fetal heart typically begin to beat detectably via ultrasound?
Which hormone, produced by the placenta, maintains the corpus luteum during early pregnancy?
What is the medical term for the condition where the placenta partially or completely covers the cervical opening?
During which trimester does the fetus typically gain the majority of its body weight?
choices: Weight gain is evenly distributed across all three

questions.q: What does the acronym HELLP syndrome stand for in the context of pregnancy complications?
choices: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
Hepatic Elevation, Endocrine Limitation, Lactose Pathway
Hemoglobin Elevation, Endothelial Loss, Placental Leakage

questions.q: At approximately how many weeks of pregnancy does quickening—the mother's first perception of fetal movement—typically occur?
Which structure, derived from embryonic tissue, becomes the umbilical cord?
What is the clinical term for abnormally high blood sugar levels during pregnancy that may develop into gestational diabetes?
In which week of pregnancy does the embryo typically become defined as a fetus?
What is the name of the protein produced during pregnancy that helps prevent the maternal immune system from attacking the fetus?
Which blood type incompatibility condition occurs when a Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus?
choices: Rh disease (hemolytic disease of the newborn)

questions.q: At what centimeter dilation does the latent phase of the first stage of labor typically transition to the active phase?
What is the medical term for when the umbilical cord wraps around the fetus, typically around the neck?
Which enzyme deficiency can lead to congenital adrenal hyperplasia, a condition that may be detected prenatally?
What is the name of the thin membrane that surrounds the amniotic sac and becomes the outer layer of the placenta?
During pregnancy, approximately what percentage increase in blood volume does a woman typically experience?
What is the medical term for the condition in which the amniotic fluid volume falls below the expected range for gestational age?
Which fetal screening test measures the thickness of fluid at the back of the fetal neck, typically performed at 11-14 weeks?
What is the name of the hormone produced by the fetus that helps soften maternal ligaments and cartilage during pregnancy?
In obstetrics, what does the Bishop score assess to determine readiness for labor?
What is the medical term for the condition where fetal hemoglobin fails to switch to adult hemoglobin after birth, potentially causing severe anemia?
During pregnancy, what is the name of the hormone produced by the corpus luteum that maintains the uterine lining during the first trimester?
Which genetic disorder is characterized by the presence of three copies of chromosome 21 and can be detected through chorionic villus sampling?
What is the threshold blood pressure reading that defines gestational hypertension during pregnancy?
In obstetrics, what does the Bishop score assess to predict the likelihood of successful vaginal delivery?
What is the name of the protein produced during pregnancy that is used as a biomarker to screen for neural tube defects?
Which maternal blood group antigen, when absent, necessitates RhoGAM administration during pregnancy to prevent alloimmunization?
What is the medical term for the condition where maternal antibodies cross the placenta and attack fetal red blood cells, causing hemolysis?
In fetal monitoring, what does the acronym VEAL CHOP represent in the context of fetal heart rate decelerations?
choices: Variable, Early, Accelerations, Late - Concerning, Head compression, Oxygenation, Placental insufficiency
Venous, Esophageal, Arterial, Lumbar - Cardiac, Hepatic, Occipital, Pulmonary
Vagal, Endocrine, Autonomic, Lactic - Coronary, Hormonal, Oxygen, Pressure
Vascular, Embryonic, Amniotic, Lingual - Cellular, Hemoglobin, Oxygen, Placental

questions.q: What is the name of the congenital condition characterized by the absence or severe underdevelopment of the abdominal muscles, resulting in a wrinkled appearance?
During the second trimester, at what week of gestation does the fetus typically develop the ability to hear maternal sounds?
What is the medical term for the reversal of blood flow in fetal vessels, often indicating severe placental dysfunction?
Which enzyme deficiency causes hemolytic disease in newborns exposed to certain medications and oxidative stress, particularly common in individuals of Mediterranean descent?
What is the name of the rare pregnancy complication where fetal tissue invades the maternal myometrium, potentially causing life-threatening hemorrhage?
In obstetrics, what does the Kleihauer-Betke test determine in the context of maternal-fetal hemorrhage?
choices: The presence of fetal antibodies in maternal blood
The volume of fetal red blood cells in maternal circulation

questions.q: During pregnancy, what is the primary function of human placental lactogen in maternal metabolism?
choices: To increase insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake
To promote lipolysis and increase maternal glucose availability for the fetus
To decrease maternal thyroid hormone production
To inhibit prolactin secretion from the pituitary gland

questions.q: What is the name of the syndrome characterized by polyhydramnios and a cardiac defect in one twin, with the other twin showing anemia and growth restriction?
In fetal echocardiography, what is the normal range for the cardiothoracic ratio in the fetus?
What is the term for the protein produced by the placenta that appears in maternal blood and urine within days of conception?
In which week of gestation does the fetal heart typically begin to beat detectably on ultrasound?
Which blood group antigen system, when absent from a pregnant person's blood, may require immunoglobulin administration after delivery?
In obstetrics, what does the Bishop score assess to determine cervical readiness?
What is the term for the condition in which a fertilized egg implants in the fallopian tube rather than the uterus?
Which anesthetic technique involves injection of medication around the spinal cord to numb lower body sensations during labor?
What is the name of the fetal measurement on ultrasound that approximates gestational age between 15-20 weeks?
In which stage of labor does the cervix dilate from 0 to 10 centimeters?
What is the condition called when maternal blood pressure elevation of 140/90 mmHg or higher occurs after 20 weeks of pregnancy without proteinuria?
Which embryonic germ layer gives rise to the neural tube, which eventually develops into the brain and spinal cord?
What is the term for the waxy, white coating that covers and protects fetal skin in utero?
At what threshold of proteinuria in a pregnant person with hypertension is preeclampsia with severe features typically diagnosed?
Which drug, a vasodilator and calcium channel blocker, is commonly used for acute hypertension management in pregnancy?
What is the name of the reflex in newborns where they turn their head toward a touch on the cheek and open their mouth?
In what week of pregnancy does the embryo officially transition to being called a fetus?
What is the condition called when excessive amniotic fluid accumulates in the uterus, typically defined as >25 cm maximum vertical pocket depth?
Which maternal serum marker measured in the second trimester, when elevated, is associated with increased risk of Down syndrome?
What is the term for the surgical removal of the uterus performed during cesarean delivery when placenta accreta is present?
Which prenatal diagnostic procedure involves inserting a needle into the amniotic sac to extract fetal cells for genetic analysis, typically performed between 15-20 weeks of gestation?

What is the medical term for the condition in which fetal hemoglobin levels are severely depleted due to maternal antibodies crossing the placental barrier, historically occurring in Rh-negative mothers carrying Rh-positive fetuses?
In obstetrics, what is the name of the thick, yellowish secretion produced by the mammary glands in the first few days after delivery, before mature milk production begins?
Which hormone, produced by the corpus luteum during early pregnancy, maintains the uterine lining and prevents menstruation until the placenta takes over production around week 12?
What is the medical term for the loss of a pregnancy before 20 weeks of gestation?
During which trimester does the fetus typically develop a heartbeat that can be detected by ultrasound?
At what cervical dilation measurement is a woman typically considered to be in active labour?
What is the term for a pregnancy in which the fertilized egg implants outside the uterus?
Which blood test screens for chromosomal abnormalities like Down syndrome in the first trimester?
What is the name of the structure that connects the fetus to the placenta?
During pregnancy, what is the term for the darkened line that often appears on the abdomen?
What is the medical term for high blood pressure that develops specifically during pregnancy?
Which hormone surge triggers the release of an egg from the ovary during the menstrual cycle?
What is the name of the protein produced by the fetus and placenta that is measured in prenatal blood tests?
At approximately how many weeks of pregnancy does the second trimester begin?
What is the medical term for when a baby is in a head-down position ready for vaginal delivery?
What condition develops when blood sugar levels become elevated during pregnancy despite no prior diabetes diagnosis?
Which scoring system evaluates a newborn's physical condition at one minute and five minutes after birth?
What is the name of the fluid-filled sac that surrounds and protects the developing fetus?
During pregnancy, the organ responsible for producing progesterone in the first weeks is which structure?
What is the medical term for the shedding of the uterine lining in the weeks following childbirth?
In which week of pregnancy is the embryonic period considered to end and the fetal period to begin?
What is the name of the hormone produced by the embryo that signals to the mother's body to maintain the uterine lining during early pregnancy?
At approximately how many weeks of pregnancy does the fetal heartbeat become detectable via doppler ultrasound?
Which prenatal screening test uses sound waves to measure fluid behind the fetal neck, typically performed between 15-20 weeks?
What is the medical term for the umbilical cord becoming wrapped around the fetal neck during pregnancy or labor?
Which hormone, produced by the placenta, helps regulate maternal blood glucose during pregnancy?
During which trimester does the fetus develop the ability to open and close its eyes?
choices: The eyes are open from conception

questions.q: What is the name of the sac that surrounds and protects the fetus, containing amniotic fluid?
Which test measures the level of a protein called AFP to screen for chromosomal abnormalities and neural tube defects?
What is the medical term for when a fetus's presenting part descends into the maternal pelvis in preparation for labor?
At approximately what week of pregnancy does the fetus develop functional taste buds?
Which hormone triggers the milk letdown reflex during breastfeeding after delivery?
What is the name of the network of blood vessels in the placenta that allows nutrient and gas exchange between mother and fetus?
Which syndrome is characterized by high blood pressure and protein in the urine developing after 20 weeks of pregnancy?
At what approximate week of pregnancy does the fetus typically transition to a head-down position in preparation for birth?
What is the medical term for the three consecutive stages of labor that culminate in the birth of the placenta?
Which type of diabetes develops during pregnancy and typically resolves after delivery?
What is the name of the reflex present at birth where a newborn turns their head when their cheek is stroked?
During pregnancy, approximately how many times does the maternal blood volume increase compared to pre-pregnancy levels?
At approximately how many weeks of pregnancy can a fetal heartbeat typically be detected via ultrasound?
When a pregnant woman experiences swelling of the hands, feet, and face due to fluid retention, what is this condition called?
What is the medical term for a pregnancy that extends beyond 42 weeks gestation?
Which fetal presentation describes a baby positioned with the head toward the mother's pelvis, ready for delivery?
What is the soft spot on an infant's skull where the bones have not yet fused together called?
During which trimester does the fetal heartbeat become audible with a standard doppler ultrasound?
What is the name of the mucus plug that seals the cervix during pregnancy and is expelled as labor approaches?
What is the hormone responsible for triggering milk production in lactating mothers called?
At approximately 20 weeks of pregnancy, a pregnant woman may experience quickening. What does this term refer to?
What is the medical procedure called when the amniotic sac is surgically removed to deliver the baby through an abdominal incision?
Which blood type classification refers to individuals who lack the Rh antigen on their red blood cells?
What is the period immediately following childbirth during which the mother's body undergoes recovery and adjustment called?
The APGAR score is assessed shortly after birth and evaluates five specific vital functions. Which of these is NOT one of them?
What is the term for when a baby moves lower into the pelvis in preparation for delivery?
Which condition occurs when a pregnant woman's blood pressure rises significantly and protein appears in her urine, potentially threatening both mother and baby?
A woman who is pregnant for the first time is referred to in medical terminology as what?
At approximately how many weeks of pregnancy does the placenta become fully functional and take over hormone production from the corpus luteum?
What is the thick, yellowish first milk produced after delivery that is rich in antibodies and nutrients called?
During pregnancy, which hormone reaches its peak levels in the third trimester and plays a crucial role in preparing the body for labour and milk production?
What is the medical term for the condition in which the placenta implants abnormally low in the uterus, potentially covering the cervix?
At approximately how many weeks of gestation can a fetal heartbeat first be detected via vaginal ultrasound?
Which of the following is the primary nutrient deficiency that, if untreated during pregnancy, can lead to neural tube defects such as spina bifida?
About how many weeks does a typical full-term pregnancy last?

source.notes: Counted from the first day of last menstrual period.

questions.q: What is the lower part of the uterus that opens during labor?

source.notes: Dilates to 10 cm during active labor.

questions.q: What's the medical name for the soft spot on a baby's head?
What's the name of the cord connecting baby to placenta?
What's the common term for the queasy nausea many pregnant women experience in the first trimester?

source.notes: Misnomer — can occur at any time of day.

questions.q: What's the spinal pain-relief injection commonly given during labor?

source.notes: Injected into the epidural space outside the spinal cord.

questions.q: What does NICU stand for in maternity hospitals?
What's the technology that uses sound waves to image the baby in utero?

source.notes: Routine scans typically at 12 weeks (dating) and 20 weeks (anatomy).

questions.q: What term describes the painful contractions during labor?

source.notes: Active labor contractions are typically 3-5 minutes apart and lasting 60-90 seconds.

questions.q: How many trimesters is a pregnancy divided into?

source.notes: Each ~13 weeks. First, second, third trimester.

questions.q: What's the formal medical term for the surgical delivery of a baby through the abdomen?

source.notes: Named for Julius Caesar (legend) — though most likely a folk etymology.

questions.q: What's the term for a non-medical professional who provides labor support?

source.notes: Greek for 'female slave' — now means trained labor support.

questions.q: Which gestational diabetes test involves drinking a sweet glucose drink?
What's the average baby weight at birth (full-term)?

source.notes: 5.5 lbs (low birth weight) to 8.8 lbs is normal range.

questions.q: What term describes a baby born before 37 weeks?
What scoring scale is used to assess a newborn's health at 1 and 5 minutes after birth?

source.notes: 0-10 scale evaluating Activity, Pulse, Grimace, Appearance, Respiration.

questions.q: What's the uterine lining shed during a period — also key to early pregnancy?
Which prenatal screening test analyzes maternal blood for fetal DNA?

source.notes: Screens for chromosomal conditions like Down syndrome. Amniocentesis is invasive (needle through abdomen).
